common_name: Fannie Munson Caladium (Classic Large Rose-Pink Fancy-Leaf Caladium)

planting.note: Plant tubers in spring after the last frost when soil has warmed to at least 65°F; plant 1–2 inches deep in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial to full shade; Fannie Munson produces large, heavy tubers over successive seasons — in zone 9, leave tubers in ground over winter with a layer of mulch protection.

directions: Plant tubers in spring after the last frost in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial to full shade; Fannie Munson is one of the most enduring and widely sold large fancy-leaf caladium varieties — a classic pink caladium with large, heart-shaped leaves bearing deep rose-pink to warm-pink centers and veining that radiates from the leaf center, semi-translucent whitish areas between veins that create a luminous, stained-glass effect, and fresh-green margins that frame and contrast the vivid pink interior pattern. The distinctive leaf pattern of Fannie Munson — the combination of deep rose-pink veins, translucent pink-white intervein areas, and the green margin — creates one of the most elegant and recognizable fancy-leaf caladium appearances, more detailed and complex than solid-colored types and more structured than randomly mottled varieties. Fannie Munson has remained in continuous commercial production for many decades because of its reliable performance, vigorous tuber production, excellent leaf quality, and universally appealing soft-pink color scheme. In zone 9, Fannie Munson is outstanding through the entire warm season (May–October), the tubers increasing in size year over year when allowed to overwinter in-ground with protective mulch. The warm-pink coloration pairs beautifully with pure white companions (white impatiens, 'Aaron' or 'Candidum' caladiums, white-flowered begonias) for a soft romantic effect, or with burgundy-to-dark-foliage companions for high contrast.

common_name: Lord Derby Caladium (Classic Pink Strap-Leaf Translucent Caladium)

planting.note: Plant tubers in spring after the last frost when soil has warmed to at least 65°F; plant 1–2 inches deep in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial to full shade; Lord Derby is a strap-leaf type with higher sun tolerance than most fancy-leaf pink caladiums — it performs well in morning-sun positions that would damage large fancy-leaf types.

directions: Plant tubers in spring after the last frost in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial shade; Lord Derby is one of the oldest continuously grown caladium cultivars — a classic strap-leaf (lance-shaped leaf) type producing elongated, narrowly heart-shaped leaves with an unusual translucent, rose-pink to deep-pink coloration that allows light to pass through the thin leaf blade, creating a jewel-like, luminous quality that is distinctly different from the opaque coloration of most other caladiums. The translucent quality of Lord Derby's leaves — similar in concept to stained glass — is most striking when backlit by morning sun or viewed against a bright sky, creating an almost glowing rose-pink effect quite unlike any other commonly grown shade plant. The strap-leaf form produces a finer-textured, more graceful display than large fancy-leaf types, and the higher sun tolerance of strap-leaf caladiums generally allows Lord Derby to be planted in positions receiving 3–4 hours of direct morning sun without the bleaching and leaf collapse that damages large white or pink fancy-leaf types. Lord Derby has been maintained in commercial production for well over a century — a testament to the unique visual quality of the translucent rose-pink coloring that has no equivalent in other modern caladium varieties. In zone 9, Lord Derby produces reliably through the warm season with tubers overwintering in-ground. Excellent in mass edging applications along shaded walks and beds.

common_name: Florida Red Ruffles Caladium (Ruffled-Margin Red Strap-Leaf Caladium)

planting.note: Plant tubers in spring after the last frost when soil has warmed to at least 65°F; plant 1–2 inches deep in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial to full shade; Florida Red Ruffles is a University of Florida caladium introduction — bred specifically for Gulf Coast conditions and performance.

directions: Plant tubers in spring after the last frost in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial to full shade; Florida Red Ruffles is a University of Florida caladium introduction distinguished by a remarkable leaf characteristic rarely found in other caladiums: distinctly ruffled, wavy, frilled leaf margins that give the foliage a crinkled, textured appearance unlike the smooth-edged leaves of most standard fancy and strap-leaf caladiums. The combination of the crimson-red to scarlet-red strap-leaf coloration and the unusually ruffled margins creates a more textured, ornamental leaf quality than any smooth-leaved red caladium — the frilling adds visual depth and interest that makes Florida Red Ruffles exceptional in close-up container plantings and detailed garden borders where the leaf texture can be appreciated. The strap-leaf form (narrower, more elongated leaves than fancy-leaf types) and compact habit produce a finer texture than large fancy-leaf varieties, making Florida Red Ruffles proportionate for small-scale applications. The ruffled margin trait is genetically stable and consistent across the crop — every leaf shows the characteristic crimping. Florida Red Ruffles performs well in the Gulf South heat and humidity for which it was bred, and the tubers overwinter successfully in-ground in zone 9b with mulch protection. Combine with smooth-leaved companions to highlight the textural contrast of the ruffled margins.

common_name: White Autumn Sage (Pure White Everblooming Salvia greggii Shrub)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained to dry, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; 'Alba' has the same excellent drought and heat tolerance as other Salvia greggii cultivars — it performs best with lean soil and minimal supplemental irrigation once established.

directions: Plant in well-drained to dry, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; 'Alba' is the pure-white flowered cultivar of Salvia greggii (autumn sage), a Texas native subshrub that produces small, two-lipped, pure-white flowers in continuous flushes from spring through hard frost. The clean white color is distinctive in the Salvia greggii palette (which otherwise runs through red, pink, coral, salmon, and bicolor combinations) — providing a true white that is uncommon among drought-tolerant Texas native salvias and outstanding for brightening shaded edges of full-sun borders, for white-and-blue combinations with blue salvias (Blue Queen, Indigo Spires), and for white garden themes. 'Alba' has the same exceptional drought tolerance, heat performance, and wildlife value as the red-flowered Salvia greggii species — the white flowers attract hummingbirds, large carpenter bees, and numerous native bee species throughout the long bloom season. The semi-evergreen to deciduous shrub reaches 2–3 feet with a similar spread and benefits from a hard cutback to 6–8 inches in late winter to promote fresh, dense growth and abundant spring flowering. In zone 9, 'Alba' reliably overwinters and produces its best flowering in the spring (March–May) and fall (September–November) shoulder seasons; summer bloom continues in the heat but may be somewhat reduced. Light shearing after each main flush encourages continuous production.

common_name: Hotspot Rose Autumn Sage (Compact Deep-Rose Salvia greggii Cultivar)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained to dry, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; the Hotspot series was selected for more compact, tidy habit than older Salvia greggii selections — making it better suited to formal border placement and smaller landscape spaces.

directions: Plant in well-drained to dry, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Hotspot Rose is a compact Salvia greggii cultivar selected for a more refined, tidy habit than older autumn sage varieties — producing the same long-season rose-pink flowers and exceptional drought and heat tolerance as standard S. greggii but on a more compact, self-shaping plant of 18–24 inches that requires less frequent hard cutting back to maintain attractive form. The deep rose-pink to cerise-pink flower color is vivid and saturated — more intensely pink than the pastel pink of some selections — and maintains its color through the Gulf South summer heat without fading. In zone 9, Hotspot Rose blooms continuously from spring through hard frost with particularly heavy flushes in spring (March–May) and fall (September–November). The compact habit makes Hotspot Rose outstanding for defined border edging, container use, and mass plantings in small residential landscape beds where the larger older selections would be out of scale. Like all Salvia greggii cultivars, Hotspot Rose is attractive to hummingbirds and native bees throughout the bloom season and highly resistant to deer browsing. The compact habit means less aggressive annual cutback is required compared to large-growing types — remove the previous year's woody stems to 6 inches in late February to promote vigorous spring growth.
